did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning algorithms, specifically CNN and Transformer blocks with a physiology-inspired module, to classify benign melanocytic nevi (junctional, compound, and intradermal) from dermoscopy images. The method achieved an average accuracy of 70.23%, outperforming human experts and baseline models.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the curation of a new dataset of 2,150 dermoscopic images, the development of a novel deep learning algorithm (physiology-inspired CNN and Transformer), and the reporting of quantitative results (70.23% accuracy) compared against human experts and baseline models. It contains standard research sections such as Methods, Experimental Settings, and Results.

title: A Deep Learning-Based Melanocytic Nevi Classification Algorithm by Leveraging Physiologic-Inspired Knowledge and Channel Encoded Information
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of benign melanocytic nevi into junctional, compound, and intradermal subtypes using dermoscopy images to facilitate early diagnosis and reduce unnecessary biopsies. The authors curated a dataset of 2,150 dermoscopic images from 1,634 subjects and proposed a deep learning algorithm leveraging physiology-inspired knowledge and channel-encoded information. The model utilizes CNN and Transformer blocks to capture local and long-range contextual features, with a specific module focusing on depth-related features from color channels. Performance was evaluated against two human dermatologists and classic CNN and Transformer baseline models using accuracy, precision, recall, and AUC metrics. The proposed method achieved an average accuracy of 70.23%, outperforming the best baseline by 3.6% and human experts by nearly 20%. The results demonstrate the clinical potential of AI in distinguishing nevus subtypes to mitigate patient harm and healthcare costs.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three subtypes of benign melanocytic nevi studied in the classification task. The dataset contained 316 images of this type. It is noted to carry a higher risk of malignancy compared to intradermal nevi.
One of the three subtypes of benign melanocytic nevi studied in the classification task. The dataset contained 612 images of this type.
One of the three subtypes of benign melanocytic nevi studied in the classification task. The dataset contained 1,222 images of this type. It is noted to pose a lower risk of malignancy.

Mentioned in the context of differential diagnosis and risk association with melanocytic nevi. Images of melanoma were included in Figure 2 from the ISIC 2018 dataset for visual comparison but were not part of the classification task or the curated dataset.

all_task_types.target: Three subtypes of benign melanocytic nevi: junctional, compound, and intradermal nevi.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is the 'classification of benign melanocytic nevi' into three discrete histological subtypes based on the depth of nevus cell nests. The model predicts one of three classes (junctional, compound, or intradermal) using dermoscopy images. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and AUC for this multi-class classification task.

datasets_used.name: Ninth People’s Hospital Affiliated to Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine Melanocytic Nevi Dataset
datasets_used.description: A curated, retrospective dataset of dermoscopic images collected from patients who underwent histopathological examination at the Ninth People’s Hospital Affiliated to Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine between January 2020 and December 2022. Images were acquired using a Taiwan CBS skin detector. The dataset contains benign melanocytic nevi classified into three subtypes based on histological gold standards.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning model (CNN and Transformer blocks) with a 9:1 split for training/testing subsets. Data augmentation (random rotation, flipping, color jittering) was applied.
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance via a five-fold cross-validation strategy. A subset of 215 images was used for comparison with human dermatologists.
use.other: Used for ablation studies and comparison against baseline models (CNN and Transformer) and human experts.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A large-scale image dataset used for pre-training the backbone network (Swin Transformer) to initialize model parameters before fine-tuning on the specific melanocytic nevi dataset.

use.training: Used for pre-training the Swin Transformer backbone network to initialize weights.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset referenced in the paper. Images from this dataset (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, vascular lesion) were used for visual comparison in Figure 2 to illustrate different skin lesions but were not part of the primary classification task or the curated dataset.

use.other: Used for visual comparison in Figure 2 and mentioned as a potential source for future data expansion.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. It is mentioned in the references and future work section as a potential source for leveraging a more diverse set of dermoscopic images.

use.other: Mentioned as a potential source for future data expansion and self-supervised learning.

datasets_used.name: BCN20000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset mentioned in the references and future work section as a potential source for leveraging a more diverse set of dermoscopic images.

use.other: Mentioned as a potential source for future data expansion and self-supervised learning.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To test the model's performance and provide a robust evaluation of the classification results.
To visualize and analyze the diagnostic performance, including precision, recall, and accuracy for each class, and to compare the proposed method against dermatologists and baseline models.

To confirm the effectiveness of specific components of the proposed algorithm (channel-specific feature extraction branch, reconstruction module, and differential loss) by comparing accuracy and AUC against baseline models.

statistical_methods_used.method: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and AUC (Area Under Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantitatively measure and compare the classification performance of the proposed deep learning algorithm, baseline models (CNN and Transformer), and human dermatologists.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy, precision, recall, and AUC metrics without statistical significance testing.

Ninth People’s Hospital Affiliated to Sh.split: Testing set (215 images, 9:1 split from 2,150 total)

ResNet50.Note: Baseline CNN model; specific accuracy not explicitly stated but implied lower than proposed method by 3.35% average accuracy improvement

EfficientNet-b4.Note: Baseline CNN model; specific metrics not explicitly stated in text, listed in Table 1/Figure 8

Ninth People’s Hospital Affiliated to Sh.notes: The proposed method outperformed human experts by nearly 20% in accuracy. Automated methods showed an overall accuracy improvement of 11.25% in CNN methods and 16.09% in transformer methods compared to dermatologists. Dermatologists tended to diagnose intradermal nevi as compound nevi. The model exhibited a recall rate of 89.6% for intradermal nevi, which was 30% higher than human experts.

architecture_used.pretraining: Swin Transformer backbone pre-trained on ImageNet

architecture_details.backbone: Swin Transformer (for the proposed method) and ResNet50/EfficientNet-b4 (for baselines)

channel_specific_branch.technique: Separable convolutions to prevent information interference between channels

reconstruction_module.structure: Three upsampling blocks and a final convolutional layer
reconstruction_module.purpose: Regularize learned features by calculating Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss with the original image

fusion_module.mechanism: Concatenates features from channel-specific and shared branches
fusion_module.normalization: CBAM module to adjust weights of important channels and spatial features

transformer_details.scaling_factor: sqrt(d) where d is the dimension

architecture_details.classification_head: MLP head mapping high-dimensional features to lesion types (3 classes)

loss_functions: Differential loss (L_diff) using cosine similarity to separate channel representations

classification_or_output_head.output_dimension: Number of classes (3: Junctional, Compound, Intradermal)

preprocessing_techniques: Random cropping at ratios from 0.5 to 1

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied at random ratios from 0.5 to 1.
Rotation angles randomly chosen between -10 and 10 degrees.

conclusions: The proposed physiology-inspired deep learning algorithm, which leverages depth-relevant, channel-specific, and channel-shared information from dermoscopy images, achieved an average classification accuracy of 70.23% for benign melanocytic nevi subtypes.
The proposed method outperformed the best-performing baseline model (Swin Transformer) by 3.6% in accuracy.
The proposed automated method outperformed two human dermatologists by nearly 20% in accuracy, demonstrating superior diagnostic performance.
Automated methods showed an overall accuracy improvement of 11.25% for CNN-based models and 16.09% for transformer-based models compared to human dermatologists.

Human dermatologists tended to misdiagnose intradermal nevi as compound nevi, whereas the proposed model exhibited a recall rate of 89.6% for intradermal nevi, which was 30% higher than that of human experts.
Ablation studies confirmed the effectiveness of the channel-specific feature extraction branch, the reconstruction module, and the differential loss in improving model robustness and accuracy.

The study represents the first attempt at artificial intelligence classification research specifically targeting benign melanocytic nevi, aiming to facilitate early diagnosis and reduce unnecessary biopsies.
The model's performance was validated on a curated dataset of 2,150 dermoscopic images from 1,634 subjects with histological gold standard labels.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single-center retrospective dataset from Shanghai, China, which may introduce population and dataset bias, limiting generalizability to other ethnicities or clinical settings.
The model was not externally validated on independent datasets; performance was evaluated using five-fold cross-validation on the curated dataset only.

The classification task involves three subtypes of benign melanocytic nevi with high inter-class similarity, making the classification challenge more difficult than typical benign/malignant binary tasks.
The dataset is imbalanced, with intradermal nevi (1,222 images) significantly outnumbering junctional nevi (316 images), which may bias the model towards the majority class.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to confirm the significance of the performance differences between the AI model and human experts.
The comparison with human experts was limited to two dermatologists with intermediate-level dermoscopy certification, which may not represent the full spectrum of clinical expertise.

The study claims to be the first AI classification research specifically targeting benign melanocytic nevi, highlighting a gap in existing literature but also indicating a lack of prior benchmarks for this specific task.

The model's accuracy (70.23%) is modest, suggesting that while it outperforms human experts in this specific setting, its clinical utility for routine diagnosis may still be limited without further improvement.
The study did not address potential data leakage risks explicitly, though a 9:1 train-test split and five-fold cross-validation were used.
The hardware used for training was an NVIDIA RTX 3090 Ti, which may not be accessible in all clinical or resource-limited settings, posing a deployment concern.
